* This is applicable when the consumer is having Kafka auto-manage group membership. If the consumer directly assigns partitions, * those partitions will never be reassigned and this callback is not applicable. *

* When Kafka is managing the group membership, a partition re-assignment will be triggered any time the members of the group change or the subscription * of the members changes. This can occur when processes die, new process instances are added or old instances come back to life after failure. * Partition re-assignments can also be triggered by changes affecting the subscribed topics (e.g. when the number of partitions is * administratively adjusted). *

* There are many uses for this functionality. One common use is saving offsets in a custom store. By saving offsets in * the {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} call we can ensure that any time partition assignment changes * the offset gets saved. *

* Another use is flushing out any kind of cache of intermediate results the consumer may be keeping. For example, * consider a case where the consumer is subscribed to a topic containing user page views, and the goal is to count the * number of page views per user for each five minute window. Let's say the topic is partitioned by the user id so that * all events for a particular user go to a single consumer instance. The consumer can keep in memory a running * tally of actions per user and only flush these out to a remote data store when its cache gets too big. However if a * partition is reassigned it may want to automatically trigger a flush of this cache, before the new owner takes over * consumption. *

* This callback will only execute in the user thread as part of the {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll(long)} call * whenever partition assignment changes. *

* Under normal conditions, if a partition is reassigned from one consumer to another, then the old consumer will * always invoke {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection) onPartitionsRevoked} for that partition prior to the new consumer * invoking {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} for the same partition. So if offsets or other state is saved in the * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection) onPartitionsRevoked} call by one consumer member, it will always be accessible by the time the * other consumer member taking over that partition and triggering its {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} callback to load the state. *

* You can think of revocation as a graceful way to give up ownership of a partition. In some cases, the consumer may not have an opportunity to do so. * For example, if the session times out, then the partitions may be reassigned before we have a chance to revoke them gracefully. * For this case, we have a third callback {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}. The difference between this function and * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} is that upon invocation of {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}, the partitions * may already be owned by some other members in the group and therefore users would not be able to commit its consumed offsets for example. * Users could implement these two functions differently (by default, * {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} will be calling {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} directly); for example, in the * {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} we should not need to store the offsets since we know these partitions are no longer owned by the consumer * at that time. *

* During a rebalance event, the {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} function will always be triggered exactly once when * the rebalance completes. That is, even if there is no newly assigned partitions for a consumer member, its {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} * will still be triggered with an empty collection of partitions. As a result this function can be used also to notify when a rebalance event has happened. * With eager rebalancing, {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} will always be called at the start of a rebalance. On the other hand, {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} * will only be called when there were non-empty partitions that were lost. * With cooperative rebalancing, {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} and {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} * will only be triggered when there are non-empty partitions revoked or lost from this consumer member during a rebalance event. *

* It is possible * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException} * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread. * Also if the callback function implementation itself throws an exception, this exception will be propagated to the current * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} as well. *

* Note that callbacks only serve as notification of an assignment change. * They cannot be used to express acceptance of the change. * Hence throwing an exception from a callback does not affect the assignment in any way, * as it will be propagated all the way up to the {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} call. * If user captures the exception in the caller, the callback is still assumed successful and no further retries will be attempted. *

* * Here is pseudo-code for a callback implementation for saving offsets: * 

 * {@code
 *   public class SaveOffsetsOnRebalance implements ConsumerRebalanceListener {
 *       private Consumer consumer;
 *
 *       public SaveOffsetsOnRebalance(Consumer consumer) {
 *           this.consumer = consumer;
 *       }
 *
 *       public void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection partitions) {
 *           // save the offsets in an external store using some custom code not described here
 *           for(TopicPartition partition: partitions)
 *              saveOffsetInExternalStore(consumer.position(partition));
 *       }
 *
 *       public void onPartitionsLost(Collection partitions) {
 *           // do not need to save the offsets since these partitions are probably owned by other consumers already
 *       }
 *
 *       public void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection partitions) {
 *           // read the offsets from an external store using some custom code not described here
 *           for(TopicPartition partition: partitions)
 *              consumer.seek(partition, readOffsetFromExternalStore(partition));
 *       }
 *   }
 * }
 *
*/ public interface ConsumerRebalanceListener { /** * A callback method the user can implement to provide handling of offset commits to a customized store. * This method will be called during a rebalance operation when the consumer has to give up some partitions. * The consumer may need to give up some partitions (thus this callback executed) under the following scenarios: *
    *
  • If the consumer assignment changes
    • *
  • If the consumer is being closed ({@link KafkaConsumer#close(CloseOptions option)})
    • *
  • If the consumer is unsubscribing ({@link KafkaConsumer#unsubscribe()})
    • *
* It is recommended that offsets should be committed in this callback to either Kafka or a * custom offset store to prevent duplicate data. *

* This callback is always called before re-assigning the partitions. * If the consumer is using the {@link GroupProtocol#CLASSIC} rebalance protocol: *

    *
  • * In eager rebalancing, onPartitionsRevoked will be called with the full set of assigned partitions as a parameter (all partitions are revoked). * It will be called even if there are no partitions to revoke. *
    • *
  • * In cooperative rebalancing, onPartitionsRevoked will be called with the set of partitions to revoke, * iff the set is non-empty. *
    • *
* If the consumer is using the {@link GroupProtocol#CONSUMER} rebalance protocol, this callback will be called * with the set of partitions to revoke iff the set is non-empty * (same behavior as the {@link GroupProtocol#CLASSIC} rebalance protocol with Cooperative mode). *

* For examples on usage of this API, see Usage Examples section of {@link KafkaConsumer KafkaConsumer}. *

* It is common for the revocation callback to use the consumer instance in order to commit offsets. It is possible * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException} * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread. * * @param partitions The list of partitions that were assigned to the consumer and now need to be revoked. This will * include the full assignment under the Classic/Eager protocol, given that it revokes all partitions. * It will only include the subset to revoke under the Classic/Cooperative and Consumer protocols. * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer} * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer} */ void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection partitions); /** * A callback method the user can implement to provide handling of customized offsets on completion of a successful * partition re-assignment. This method will be called after the partition re-assignment completes (even if no new * partitions were assigned to the consumer), and before the consumer starts fetching data, * and only as the result of a {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll(long)} call. *

* It is guaranteed that under normal conditions all the processes in a consumer group will execute their * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} callback before any instance executes this onPartitionsAssigned callback. * During exceptional scenarios, partitions may be migrated * without the old owner being notified (i.e. their {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} callback not triggered), * and later when the old owner consumer realized this event, the {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} callback * will be triggered by the consumer then. *

* It is common for the assignment callback to use the consumer instance in order to query offsets. It is possible * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException} * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread. * * @param partitions Partitions that have been added to the assignment as a result of the rebalance. * Note that partitions that were already owned by this consumer and remain assigned are not * included in this list under the Classic/Cooperative or Consumer protocols. THe full assignment * will be received under the Classic/Eager protocol. * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer} * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer} */ void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection partitions); /** * A callback method you can implement to provide handling of cleaning up resources for partitions that have already * been reassigned to other consumers. This method will not be called during normal execution as the owned partitions would * first be revoked by calling the {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked}, before being reassigned * to other consumers during a rebalance event. However, during exceptional scenarios when the consumer realized that it * does not own this partition any longer, i.e. not revoked via a normal rebalance event, then this method would be invoked. *

* For example, this function is called if a consumer's session timeout has expired, or if a fatal error has been * received indicating the consumer is no longer part of the group. *

* By default it will just trigger {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked}; for users who want to distinguish * the handling logic of revoked partitions v.s. lost partitions, they can override the default implementation. *

* It is possible * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException} * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread. * * @param partitions The list of partitions that were assigned to the consumer and now have been reassigned * to other consumers. results.content:
300→ *
  • If the process itself is highly available and will be restarted if it fails (perhaps using a 301→ * cluster management framework like YARN, Mesos, or AWS facilities, or as part of a stream processing framework). In 302→ * this case there is no need for Kafka to detect the failure and reassign the partition since the consuming process 303→ * will be restarted on another machine. 304→ * 305→ *

    306→ * To use this mode, instead of subscribing to the topic using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}, you just call 307→ * {@link #assign(Collection)} with the full list of partitions that you want to consume. 308→ * 309→ * 

       310→ * {@code
   311→ *     String topic = "foo";
   312→ *     TopicPartition partition0 = new TopicPartition(topic, 0);
   313→ *     TopicPartition partition1 = new TopicPartition(topic, 1);
   314→ *     consumer.assign(Arrays.asList(partition0, partition1));
   315→ * }
    316→ * 317→ * Once assigned, you can call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} in a loop, just as in the preceding examples to consume 318→ * records. The group that the consumer specifies is still used for committing offsets, but now the set of partitions 319→ * will only change with another call to {@link #assign(Collection) assign}. Manual partition assignment does 320→ * not use group coordination, so consumer failures will not cause assigned partitions to be rebalanced. Each consumer 321→ * acts independently even if it shares a groupId with another consumer. To avoid offset commit conflicts, you should 322→ * usually ensure that the groupId is unique for each consumer instance. 323→ *

    324→ * Note that it isn't possible to mix manual partition assignment (i.e. using {@link #assign(Collection) assign}) 325→ * with dynamic partition assignment through topic subscription (i.e. using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}). 326→ * 327→ *

    Storing Offsets Outside Kafka

    328→ * 329→ * The consumer application need not use Kafka's built-in offset storage, it can store offsets in a store of its own 330→ * choosing. The primary use case for this is allowing the application to store both the offset and the results of the 331→ * consumption in the same system in a way that both the results and offsets are stored atomically. This is not always 332→ * possible, but when it is it will make the consumption fully atomic and give "exactly once" semantics that are 333→ * stronger than the default "at-least once" semantics you get with Kafka's offset commit functionality. 334→ *

    335→ * Here are a couple of examples of this type of usage: 336→ *

      337→ *
    • If the results of the consumption are being stored in a relational database, storing the offset in the database 338→ * as well can allow committing both the results and offset in a single transaction. Thus either the transaction will 339→ * succeed and the offset will be updated based on what was consumed or the result will not be stored and the offset 340→ * won't be updated. 341→ *
    • If the results are being stored in a local store it may be possible to store the offset there as well. For 342→ * example a search index could be built by subscribing to a particular partition and storing both the offset and the 343→ * indexed data together. If this is done in a way that is atomic, it is often possible to have it be the case that even 344→ * if a crash occurs that causes unsync'd data to be lost, whatever is left has the corresponding offset stored as well. 345→ * This means that in this case the indexing process that comes back having lost recent updates just resumes indexing 346→ * from what it has ensuring that no updates are lost. 347→ *
    348→ *

    349→ * Each record comes with its own offset, so to manage your own offset you just need to do the following: 350→ * 351→ *

      352→ *
    • Configure enable.auto.commit=false 353→ *
    • Use the offset provided with each {@link ConsumerRecord} to save your position. 354→ *
    • On restart restore the position of the consumer using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)}. 355→ *
    356→ * 357→ *

    358→ * This type of usage is simplest when the partition assignment is also done manually (this would be likely in the 359→ * search index use case described above). If the partition assignment is done automatically special care is 360→ * needed to handle the case where partition assignments change. This can be done by providing a 361→ * {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} instance in the call to {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)} 362→ * and {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}. 363→ * For example, when partitions are taken from a consumer the consumer will want to commit its offset for those partitions by 364→ * implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)}. When partitions are assigned to a 365→ * consumer, the consumer will want to look up the offset for those new partitions and correctly initialize the consumer 366→ * to that position by implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)}. 367→ *

    368→ * Another common use for {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} is to flush any caches the application maintains for 369→ * partitions that are moved elsewhere. 370→ * 371→ *

    Controlling The Consumer's Position

    372→ * 373→ * In most use cases the consumer will simply consume records from beginning to end, periodically committing its 374→ * position (either automatically or manually). However Kafka allows the consumer to manually control its position, 375→ * moving forward or backwards in a partition at will. This means a consumer can re-consume older records, or skip to 376→ * the most recent records without actually consuming the intermediate records. 377→ *

    378→ * There are several instances where manually controlling the consumer's position can be useful. 379→ *

    380→ * One case is for time-sensitive record processing it may make sense for a consumer that falls far enough behind to not 381→ * attempt to catch up processing all records, but rather just skip to the most recent records. 382→ *

    383→ * Another use case is for a system that maintains local state as described in the previous section.     • If the results of the consumption are being stored in a relational database, storing the offset in the database\n * as well can allow committing both the results and offset in a single transaction.     • If the results are being stored in a local store it may be possible to store the offset there as well.     316→ * 317→ * Once assigned, you can call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} in a loop, just as in the preceding examples to consume 318→ * records. The group that the consumer specifies is still used for committing offsets, but now the set of partitions 319→ * will only change with another call to {@link #assign(Collection) assign}. Manual partition assignment does 320→ * not use group coordination, so consumer failures will not cause assigned partitions to be rebalanced. Each consumer 321→ * acts independently even if it shares a groupId with another consumer. To avoid offset commit conflicts, you should 322→ * usually ensure that the groupId is unique for each consumer instance. 323→ *

    324→ * Note that it isn't possible to mix manual partition assignment (i.e. using {@link #assign(Collection) assign}) 325→ * with dynamic partition assignment through topic subscription (i.e. using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}). 326→ * 327→ *

    Storing Offsets Outside Kafka

    328→ * 329→ * The consumer application need not use Kafka's built-in offset storage, it can store offsets in a store of its own 330→ * choosing. The primary use case for this is allowing the application to store both the offset and the results of the 331→ * consumption in the same system in a way that both the results and offsets are stored atomically. This is not always 332→ * possible, but when it is it will make the consumption fully atomic and give "exactly once" semantics that are 333→ * stronger than the default "at-least once" semantics you get with Kafka's offset commit functionality. 334→ *

    335→ * Here are a couple of examples of this type of usage: 336→ *

      337→ *
    • If the results of the consumption are being stored in a relational database, storing the offset in the database 338→ * as well can allow committing both the results and offset in a single transaction. Thus either the transaction will 339→ * succeed and the offset will be updated based on what was consumed or the result will not be stored and the offset 340→ * won't be updated. 341→ *
    • If the results are being stored in a local store it may be possible to store the offset there as well. For 342→ * example a search index could be built by subscribing to a particular partition and storing both the offset and the 343→ * indexed data together. If this is done in a way that is atomic, it is often possible to have it be the case that even 344→ * if a crash occurs that causes unsync'd data to be lost, whatever is left has the corresponding offset stored as well. 345→ * This means that in this case the indexing process that comes back having lost recent updates just resumes indexing 346→ * from what it has ensuring that no updates are lost. 347→ *
    348→ *

    349→ * Each record comes with its own offset, so to manage your own offset you just need to do the following: 350→ * 351→ *

      352→ *
    • Configure enable.auto.commit=false 353→ *
    • Use the offset provided with each {@link ConsumerRecord} to save your position. 354→ *
    • On restart restore the position of the consumer using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)}. 355→ *
    356→ * 357→ *

    358→ * This type of usage is simplest when the partition assignment is also done manually (this would be likely in the 359→ * search index use case described above). If the partition assignment is done automatically special care is 360→ * needed to handle the case where partition assignments change. This can be done by providing a 361→ * {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} instance in the call to {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)} 362→ * and {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}. 363→ * For example, when partitions are taken from a consumer the consumer will want to commit its offset for those partitions by 364→ * implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)}. When partitions are assigned to a 365→ * consumer, the consumer will want to look up the offset for those new partitions and correctly initialize the consumer 366→ * to that position by implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)}. 367→ *

    368→ * Another common use for {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} is to flush any caches the application maintains for 369→ * partitions that are moved elsewhere. 370→ * 371→ *

    Controlling The Consumer's Position

    372→ * 373→ * In most use cases the consumer will simply consume records from beginning to end, periodically committing its 374→ * position (either automatically or manually). However Kafka allows the consumer to manually control its position, 375→ * moving forward or backwards in a partition at will. This means a consumer can re-consume older records, or skip to 376→ * the most recent records without actually consuming the intermediate records. 377→ *

    378→ * There are several instances where manually controlling the consumer's position can be useful. 379→ *

    380→ * One case is for time-sensitive record processing it may make sense for a consumer that falls far enough behind to not 381→ * attempt to catch up processing all records, but rather just skip to the most recent records. 382→ *

    383→ * Another use case is for a system that maintains local state as described in the previous section. In such a system 384→ * the consumer will want to initialize its position on start-up to whatever is contained in the local store. Likewise 385→ * if the local state is destroyed (say because the disk is lost) the state may be recreated on a new machine by 386→ * re-consuming all the data and recreating the state (assuming that Kafka is retaining sufficient history). 387→ *

    388→ * Kafka allows specifying the position using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)} to specify the new position. Special 389→ * methods for seeking to the earliest and latest offset the server maintains are also available ( 390→ * {@link #seekToBeginning(Collection)} and {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} respectively). 391→ * 392→ *

    Consumption Flow Control

    393→ * 394→ * If a consumer is assigned multiple partitions to fetch data from, it will try to consume from all of them at the same time, 395→ * effectively giving these partitions the same priority for consumption. However in some cases consumers may want to 396→ * first focus on fetching from some subset of the assigned partitions at full speed, and only start fetching other partitions 397→ * when these partitions have few or no data to consume. 398→ * 399→ *

    400→ * One of such cases is stream processing, where processor fetches from two topics and performs the join on these two streams. 401→ * When one of the topics is long lagging behind the other, the processor would like to pause fetching from the ahead topic 402→ * in order to get the lagging stream to catch up. Another example is bootstrapping upon consumer starting up where there are 403→ * a lot of history data to catch up, the applications usually want to get the latest data on some of the topics before consider 404→ * fetching other topics. 405→ * 406→ *

    407→ * Kafka supports dynamic controlling of consumption flows by using {@link #pause(Collection)} and {@link #resume(Collection)} 408→ * to pause the consumption on the specified assigned partitions and resume the consumption 409→ * on the specified paused partitions respectively in the future {@link #poll(Duration)} calls. 410→ * 411→ *

    Reading Transactional Messages

    412→ * 413→ *

    414→ * Transactions were introduced in Kafka 0.11.0 wherein applications can write to multiple topics and partitions atomically. 415→ * In order for this to work, consumers reading from these partitions should be configured to only read committed data. 416→ * This can be achieved by setting the {@code isolation.level=read_committed} in the consumer's configuration. 417→ * 418→ *

    419→ * In read_committed mode, the consumer will read only those transactional messages which have been 420→ * successfully committed. It will continue to read non-transactional messages as before. There is no client-side 421→ * buffering in read_committed mode. Instead, the end offset of a partition for a read_committed 422→ * consumer would be the offset of the first message in the partition belonging to an open transaction. This offset 423→ * is known as the 'Last Stable Offset'(LSO).

    424→ * 425→ *

    426→ * A {@code read_committed} consumer will only read up to the LSO and filter out any transactional 427→ * messages which have been aborted. The LSO also affects the behavior of {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} and 428→ * {@link #endOffsets(Collection)} for {@code read_committed} consumers, details of which are in each method's documentation. 429→ * Finally, the fetch lag metrics are also adjusted to be relative to the LSO for {@code read_committed} consumers. 430→ * 431→ *

    432→ * Partitions with transactional messages will include commit or abort markers which indicate the result of a transaction. 433→ * There markers are not returned to applications, yet have an offset in the log. As a result, applications reading from 434→ * topics with transactional messages will see gaps in the consumed offsets. These missing messages would be the transaction 435→ * markers, and they are filtered out for consumers in both isolation levels. Additionally, applications using 436→ * {@code read_committed} consumers may also see gaps due to aborted transactions, since those messages would not 437→ * be returned by the consumer and yet would have valid offsets. 438→ * 439→ *

    Multi-threaded Processing

    440→ * 441→ * The Kafka consumer is NOT thread-safe. It is the responsibility of the user to ensure that multi-threaded access 442→ * is properly synchronized. Un-synchronized access will result in {@link ConcurrentModificationException}. 443→ * 444→ *

    445→ * The only exception to this rule is {@link #wakeup()}, which can safely be used from an external thread to 446→ * interrupt an active operation. In this case, a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} will be 447→ * thrown from the thread blocking on the operation. This can be used to shutdown the consumer from another thread. 448→ * The following snippet shows the typical pattern: 449→ * 450→ * 

       451→ * {@code
   452→ * public class KafkaConsumerRunner implements Runnable {
   453→ *     private final AtomicBoolean closed = new AtomicBoolean(false);
   454→ *     private final KafkaConsumer consumer;
   455→ *
   456→ *     public KafkaConsumerRunner(KafkaConsumer consumer) {
   457→ *       this.consumer = consumer;
   458→ *     }
   459→ *
   460→ *     @Override
   461→ *     public void run() {
   462→ *         try {
   463→ *             consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList("topic"));
   464→ *             while (!closed.get()) {
   465→ *                 ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(10000));
   466→ *                 // Handle new records
   467→ *             }
   468→ *         } catch (WakeupException e) {
   469→ *             // Ignore exception if closing
   470→ *             if (!closed.get()) throw e;
   471→ *         } finally {
   472→ *             consumer.close();
   473→ *         }
   474→ *     }
   475→ *
   476→ *     // Shutdown hook which can be called from a separate thread
   477→ *     public void shutdown() {
   478→ *         closed.set(true);
   479→ *         consumer.wakeup();
   480→ *     }
   481→ * }
   482→ * }
    483→ * 484→ * Then in a separate thread, the consumer can be shutdown by setting the closed flag and waking up the consumer. 485→ * 486→ *

    487→ * 

       488→ * {@code
   489→ *     closed.set(true);
   490→ *     consumer.wakeup();
   491→ * }
    492→ * 493→ *

    494→ * Note that while it is possible to use thread interrupts instead of {@link #wakeup()} to abort a blocking operation 495→ * (in which case, {@link InterruptException} will be raised), we discourage their use since they may cause a clean 496→ * shutdown of the consumer to be aborted. Interrupts are mainly supported for those cases where using {@link #wakeup()} 497→ * is impossible, e.g. when a consumer thread is managed by code that is unaware of the Kafka client. 498→ * 499→ *

The LSO also affects the behavior of {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} and 428→ * {@link #endOffsets(Collection)} for {@code read_committed} consumers, details of which are in each method's documentation. 429→ * Finally, the fetch lag metrics are also adjusted to be relative to the LSO for {@code read_committed} consumers. 430→ * 431→ *

As a result, applications reading from 434→ * topics with transactional messages will see gaps in the consumed offsets. These missing messages would be the transaction 435→ * markers, and they are filtered out for consumers in both isolation levels. Additionally, applications using 436→ * {@code read_committed} consumers may also see gaps due to aborted transactions, since those messages would not 437→ * be returned by the consumer and yet would have valid offsets. 438→ * 439→ *

This can be used to shutdown the consumer from another thread. 448→ * The following snippet shows the typical pattern: 449→ * 450→ * 

       451→ * {@code
   452→ * public class KafkaConsumerRunner implements Runnable {

   454→ *     private final KafkaConsumer consumer;
   455→ *
   456→ *     public KafkaConsumerRunner(KafkaConsumer consumer) {
   457→ *       this.consumer = consumer;
   458→ *     }
   459→ *
   460→ *     @Override
   461→ *     public void run() {
   462→ *         try {
   463→ *             consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList("topic"));
   464→ *             while (!closed.get()) {
   465→ *                 ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(10000));
   466→ *                 // Handle new records
   467→ *             }
   468→ *         } catch (WakeupException e) {
   469→ *             // Ignore exception if closing
   470→ *             if (!closed.get()) throw e;
   471→ *         } finally {
   472→ *             consumer.close();
   473→ *         }
   474→ *     }
   475→ *
   476→ *     // Shutdown hook which can be called from a separate thread
   477→ *     public void shutdown() {
   478→ *         closed.set(true);
   479→ *         consumer.wakeup();
   480→ *     }
   481→ * }
   482→ * }
    483→ * 484→ * Then in a separate thread, the consumer can be shutdown by setting the closed flag and waking up the consumer. 485→ * 486→ *

    487→ * 

       488→ * {@code
   489→ *     closed.set(true);
   490→ *     consumer.wakeup();
   491→ * }
    492→ * 493→ *

    494→ * Note that while it is possible to use thread interrupts instead of {@link #wakeup()} to abort a blocking operation 495→ * (in which case, {@link InterruptException} will be raised), we discourage their use since they may cause a clean 496→ * shutdown of the consumer to be aborted. Interrupts are mainly supported for those cases where using {@link #wakeup()} 497→ * is impossible, e.g. when a consumer thread is managed by code that is unaware of the Kafka client. 498→ * 499→ *

/* * Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one or more * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership. * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0 * (the "License"); you may not use this file except in compliance with * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at * * http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0 * * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software * distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS, * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied. * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and * limitations under the License. */ package org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer; import org.apache.kafka.common.header.Headers; import org.apache.kafka.common.header.internals.RecordHeaders; import org.apache.kafka.common.record.TimestampType; import org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal.RecordBatch; import java.util.ConcurrentModificationException; import java.util.Optional; /** * A key/value pair to be received from Kafka. This also consists of a topic name and * a partition number from which the record is being received, an offset that points * to the record in a Kafka partition, and a timestamp as marked by the corresponding ProducerRecord. *

* *

Thread Safety

* This consumer record is not thread-safe. Concurrent access to a {@code ConsumerRecord} instance by * multiple threads may result in undefined behavior, including but not limited to the following: *
    *
  • Throwing {@link ConcurrentModificationException} (e.g., when concurrently modifying {@link #headers()}).
    • *
  • Data corruption or logical errors (e.g., inconsistent state of {@code headers} or {@code value}).
    • *
  • Visibility issues (e.g., modifications by one thread not being visible to another thread).
    • *
* *

* In particular, the {@link #headers()} method returns a mutable collection of headers. If multiple * threads access or modify these headers concurrently, it may lead to race conditions or inconsistent * states. It is the responsibility of the user to ensure that multi-threaded access is properly synchronized. * *

* However, each individual {@link org.apache.kafka.common.header.Header} instance * is read thread-safe; that is, it is safe for multiple threads to read the same header's key or value concurrently * as long as no thread modifies it.

/** * Get the set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer. If subscription happened by directly assigning * partitions using {@link #assign(Collection)} then this will simply return the same partitions that * were assigned. If topic subscription was used, then this will give the set of topic partitions currently assigned * to the consumer (which may be none if the assignment hasn't happened yet, or the partitions are in the * process of getting reassigned). * @return The set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer */ public Set assignment() { return delegate.assignment(); } /** * Get the current subscription. Will return the same topics used in the most recent call to * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, or an empty set if no such call has been made. * @return The set of topics currently subscribed to */ public Set subscription() { return delegate.subscription(); } /** * Subscribe to the given list of topics to get dynamically * assigned partitions. Topic subscriptions are not incremental. This list will replace the current * assignment (if there is one). Note that it is not possible to combine topic subscription with group management * with manual partition assignment through {@link #assign(Collection)}. * * If the given list of topics is empty, it is treated the same as {@link #unsubscribe()}. * *

* As part of group management, the group coordinator will keep track of the list of consumers that belong to a particular * group and will trigger a rebalance operation if any one of the following events are triggered: *

    *

  • A subscribed topic is created or deleted *
  • An existing member of the consumer group is shutdown or fails *
  • A new member is added to the consumer group *
*

* When any of these events are triggered, the provided listener will be invoked in this way: *

    *
  • {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} will be invoked with the partitions to revoke, before re-assigning those partitions to another consumer.
    • *
  • {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)} will be invoked when the rebalance completes (even if no new partitions are assigned to the consumer)
    • *
* Note that rebalances will only occur during an active call to {@link #poll(Duration)}, so callbacks will * also only be invoked during that time. * * The provided listener will immediately override any listener set in a previous call to subscribe. * It is guaranteed, however, that the partitions revoked/assigned through this interface are from topics * subscribed in this call. This list will replace the current * assignment (if there is one). It is not possible to combine topic subscription with group management * with manual partition assignment through {@link #assign(Collection)}. * * If the given list of topics is empty, it is treated the same as {@link #unsubscribe()}. * *

* This is a short-hand for {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, which * uses a no-op listener. If you need the ability to seek to particular offsets, you should prefer * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, since group rebalances will cause partition offsets * to be reset. 958→ * This commits offsets only to Kafka. The offsets committed using this API will be used on the first fetch after 959→ * every rebalance and also on startup. As such, if you need to store offsets in anything other than Kafka, this API 960→ * should not be used. 961→ *

/*

 * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with
 * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.
 * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0
 * (the "License"); you may not use this file except in compliance with
 * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at
 *
 *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
 *
 * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
 * distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
 * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
 * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
 * limitations under the License.
 */
package org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer;

import org.apache.kafka.common.TopicPartition;

import java.time.Duration;
import java.util.Collection;
import java.util.Map;

/**
 * A callback interface that the user can implement to trigger custom actions when a commit request completes. The callback
 * may be executed in any thread calling {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll()}.
 */
@FunctionalInterface
public interface OffsetCommitCallback {

    /**
     * A callback method the user can implement to provide asynchronous handling of commit request completion.
     * This method will be called when the commit request sent to the server has been acknowledged.
     *
     * @param offsets A map of the offsets and associated metadata that this callback applies to
     * @param exception The exception thrown during processing of the request, or null if the commit completed successfully
     *
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CommitFailedException if the commit failed and cannot be retried.
     *             This can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link KafkaConsumer#subscribe(Collection)},
     *             or if there is an active group with the same groupId which is using group management.
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the commit failed because
     *            it is in the middle of a rebalance. In such cases
     *            commit could be retried after the rebalance is completed with the {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(Duration)} call.
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link KafkaConsumer#wakeup()} is called before or while this
     *             function is called
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while
     *             this function is called
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the
     *             configured groupId. See the exception for more details
     * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. if offset metadata
     *             is too large or if the committed offset is invalid).
     */
    void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception);
}

arguments.content:
# Apache Kafka KafkaConsumer API Reference Documentation

## Table of Contents

1. [Overview](#overview)

## Overview

### Threading Model

The Kafka KafkaConsumer is **NOT thread-safe**. All consumer operations must occur within a single consumer thread. The only exception is `wakeup()`, which is explicitly designed to be called from external threads to interrupt blocking operations.

**Key Threading Points:**
- `KafkaConsumer` maintains an internal event loop that processes rebalances, heartbeats, and data fetches
- All callbacks (ConsumerRebalanceListener, OffsetCommitCallback) execute within the user's calling thread
- Concurrent access without synchronization results in `ConcurrentModificationException`
- The `wakeup()` method is the only safe cross-thread operation

### Consumer Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Application Thread                         │
│  (calls poll(), subscribe(), seek(), etc.)  │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
                   │
        ┌──────────▼──────────┐
        │   KafkaConsumer     │
        │  (Delegation Layer) │
        └──────────────────┬──┘
                           │
        ┌──────────────────▼──────────────┐
        │  ConsumerDelegate               │
        │  (Classic/Async Protocol)       │
        │  - Rebalance Coordinator        │
        │  - Fetcher                      │
        │  - Heartbeat Thread             │
        │  - Metrics                      │
        └──────────────────────────────────┘
```

The `KafkaConsumer` delegates to a protocol-specific implementation (`ClassicKafkaConsumer` or `AsyncKafkaConsumer`), which manages:
- Group coordination and rebalancing
- Heartbeat sending to the broker
- Fetching records in batches
- Offset tracking and commits

---

## Core Types

### KafkaConsumer<K, V>

The main consumer class. Generic over key and value types with deserializers specified in configuration.

```java
// Constructor variants
KafkaConsumer(Map<String, Object> configs)
KafkaConsumer(Properties properties)
KafkaConsumer(Properties properties, Deserializer<K> keyDeserializer, Deserializer<V> valueDeserializer)
KafkaConsumer(Map<String, Object> configs, Deserializer<K> keyDeserializer, Deserializer<V> valueDeserializer)
```

### ConsumerRecord<K, V>

Represents a single record retrieved from Kafka.

**Key Fields:**
- `topic()`: Topic name
- `partition()`: Partition ID
- `offset()`: Offset within the partition (strictly increasing but may have gaps)
- `timestamp()`: Message timestamp
- `timestampType()`: Type of timestamp (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME)
- `key()`, `value()`: Deserialized key and value
- `headers()`: Record headers (mutable collection)
- `leaderEpoch()`: Optional leader epoch of the record

**Thread Safety Note:** ConsumerRecord is NOT thread-safe. Concurrent access by multiple threads may result in `ConcurrentModificationException` or data corruption.

### ConsumerRecords<K, V>

Container holding records from a `poll()` call. Partitioned by `TopicPartition`.

**Key Methods:**
- `records(TopicPartition)`: List of records for a partition
- `records(String topic)`: Iterable of all records for a topic
- `partitions()`: Set of partitions with data
- `nextOffsets()`: Map of next offset and metadata for each partition (the offset to commit)
- `count()`: Total number of records
- `isEmpty()`: Whether the collection is empty
- `iterator()`: Iterate across all records

**Important:** Use `nextOffsets()` when committing offsets after processing, as it contains the correct offset to commit (next record to be read).

### ConsumerRebalanceListener

Callback interface for rebalance events. Must be provided to `subscribe()` for proper offset and state management.

**Methods:**
- `onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition>)`: Called when partitions are revoked (before rebalance)
- `onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition>)`: Called when partitions are assigned (after rebalance)
- `onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition>)`: Called when partitions are lost without revocation (default delegates to onPartitionsRevoked)

**Execution:** Always called from within the user thread as part of `poll()` during rebalancing.

### OffsetAndMetadata

Represents a committed offset with optional metadata.

```java
public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset)
public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, String metadata)
public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, Optional<Integer> leaderEpoch, String metadata)
```

**Key Points:**
- The offset should be the **next offset to read** (one past the last processed message)
- Leader epoch is optional metadata used for fencing and safety
- Metadata is user-defined and returned on fetch

### OffsetCommitCallback

Functional interface for handling async commit completions.

```java
void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception);
```

Called when a `commitAsync()` request completes, with either success (`exception == null`) or failure.

### GroupProtocol

Enum specifying the group coordination protocol:
- `CLASSIC`: Original Kafka protocol (eager or cooperative rebalancing)
- `CONSUMER`: New unified consumer group protocol (always cooperative)

---

## Consumer Lifecycle

### Construction and Initialization

```java
Properties props = new Properties();
props.setProperty("bootstrap.servers", "localhost:9092");
props.setProperty("group.id", "my-group");
props.setProperty("key.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");
props.setProperty("value.deserializer", "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer");

KafkaConsumer<String, String> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(props);
```

**During Construction:**
- Deserializers are instantiated and configured
- Metrics are initialized
- Consumer does NOT immediately join the group (deferred to first `poll()` call)

### Subscription Phase

```java
consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList("topic1", "topic2"));
// or with listener:
consumer.subscribe(
    Arrays.asList("topic1", "topic2"),
    new MyRebalanceListener()
);
```

**Behavior:**
- Subscription is non-incremental (replaces previous subscription)
- Does not immediately join the group
- Metadata fetching for subscribed topics begins in the background
- Empty subscription list is equivalent to `unsubscribe()`

### Group Membership Join

The consumer joins the group on the **first `poll()` call**, not during `subscribe()`.

```java
// Group join happens here:
ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
```

**Join Process:**
1. Metadata refresh to find broker
2. FindCoordinator request to locate the group coordinator
3. JoinGroup request to join the group
4. SyncGroup request to receive partition assignment
5. Rebalance callbacks invoked (if listener provided)

### Closing and Resource Cleanup

```java
consumer.close();
// or with timeout:
consumer.close(Duration.ofSeconds(30));
// or with options:
consumer.close(CloseOptions.builder().timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(30)).build());
```

**Close Behavior:**
- Revokes assigned partitions (calls `onPartitionsRevoked()` on listener)
- Commits offsets if `enable.auto.commit` is true and offset tracking is enabled
- Closes all broker connections
- Interrupts all internal threads
- Timeout applies to graceful shutdown; after timeout, unclean shutdown occurs

---

## Subscription and Assignment

### Dynamic Assignment via Subscription

**Methods:**
```java
// List of topics
void subscribe(Collection<String> topics)
void subscribe(Collection<String> topics, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener)

// Pattern matching (periodic refresh)
void subscribe(Pattern pattern)
void subscribe(Pattern pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener)

// Google RE2/J compatible pattern (CONSUMER protocol only)
void subscribe(SubscriptionPattern pattern)
void subscribe(SubscriptionPattern pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener)
```

**Key Characteristics:**
- Enables automatic load balancing across consumer group members
- Rebalancing occurs when:
  - Partitions added to/removed from subscribed topics
  - Consumer joins/leaves group
  - Consumer crashes/disconnects
- Rebalancing is **only detected and triggered during `poll()`**
- Pattern subscriptions refresh metadata periodically (controlled by `metadata.max.age.ms`)

**Incompatibility:** Cannot mix `subscribe()` and `assign()` without calling `unsubscribe()` first.

### Manual Assignment

**Method:**
```java
void assign(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
```

**Characteristics:**
- Static partition assignment without group coordination
- Suitable for:
  - Stateful processing where local state is associated with partitions
  - Standalone consumers that don't need rebalancing
  - Custom assignment logic
- Partition changes only occur on explicit `assign()` calls
- Offset management still uses group membership for commits (but can be standalone)

**Example:**
```java
consumer.assign(Arrays.asList(
    new TopicPartition("topic", 0),
    new TopicPartition("topic", 1)
));
```

### Unsubscribe

```java
void unsubscribe()
```

Removes all subscriptions and assignments. The next `poll()` will return immediately with no records.

---

## Polling and Data Fetching

### poll(Duration timeout)

```java
ConsumerRecords<K, V> poll(Duration timeout)
```

**Semantics:**
- Fetches records from assigned partitions
- Returns immediately if records are available
- Blocks for up to `timeout` duration if no records available
- **May block longer than timeout** if a rebalance is triggered
- Executes rebalance callbacks if rebalancing occurs
- Sends heartbeats to maintain group membership
- Automatically refreshes metadata when needed
- Can throw `WakeupException` if `wakeup()` called

**Return Value:**
- `ConsumerRecords` containing fetched records partitioned by `TopicPartition`
- Empty `ConsumerRecords` if no data available (timeout expired, or empty assignment)
- Records from paused partitions are excluded

**Important Behaviors:**

1. **Rebalance Callbacks During Poll**
   - If a rebalance is detected, callbacks execute **within the `poll()` call**
   - Rebalance can occur at any point during poll processing
   - Callbacks may block arbitrary durations
   - `poll()` only returns after rebalance completes

2. **Max Records Limiting**
   - `max.poll.records` configuration limits the batch size
   - Useful for predictable processing time windows
   - Default is 500 records

3. **Offset Advancement**
   - Position advances as records are returned
   - Position = offset of next record to be returned
   - NOT advanced if record is returned but not processed
   - Automatic commits occur relative to position

### poll() Loop Requirements

To maintain group membership, you **MUST call `poll()` frequently**:
- At least once every `max.poll.interval.ms` (default: 5 minutes)
- If not called within this interval, the consumer proactively leaves the group
- Subsequent `commitSync()` calls fail with `CommitFailedException`

```java
// RECOMMENDED: Keep poll interval tight for responsiveness
try {
    while (true) {
        ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
        for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : records) {
            // Fast processing only
            processRecord(record);
        }
        consumer.commitSync();
    }
} catch (WakeupException e) {
    // Expected on shutdown
} finally {
    consumer.close();
}
```

---

## Offset Management

### Offset Concepts

**Position** (consumer's current position):
- Offset of the next record to be fetched
- Automatically advanced by `poll()`
- Can be queried with `position(TopicPartition)`
- Can be moved with `seek(TopicPartition, long)`

**Committed Offset**:
- Last offset stored in the broker for the partition
- Used as the starting position on consumer restart
- Committed via `commitSync()` or `commitAsync()`
- Queried with `committed(Set<TopicPartition>)`

**Relationship:**
- Committed offset should be **next offset to read** (one past the last processed message)
- If position > committed: records haven't been committed yet
- If position < committed: some records were skipped

### Offset Commit Strategies

#### Automatic Committing

**Configuration:**
```properties
enable.auto.commit=true
auto.commit.interval.ms=1000
```

**Behavior:**
- Offsets committed periodically (every `auto.commit.interval.ms`)
- Committed offset = current position at commit time
- Non-blocking (background operation)
- Risk: Can commit offsets before records are processed

**Suitable For:**
- Simple use cases with fast, reliable processing
- "At-least-once" delivery with automatic retry

#### Synchronous Commits

```java
consumer.commitSync()
consumer.commitSync(Duration.ofSeconds(10))
consumer.commitSync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets)
consumer.commitSync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Duration timeout)
```

**Behavior:**
- Blocks until commit succeeds or fails
- Retries transient failures
- Throws `CommitFailedException` if consumer lost group membership
- Throws `TimeoutException` if operation exceeds timeout
- May fail with `RebalanceInProgressException` during rebalance

**Suitable For:**
- Exact-once or at-least-once processing with external state
- Batched processing where records are buffered before commit
- Custom offset stores

**Best Practice:**
```java
while (running) {
    ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
    List<String> batch = new ArrayList<>();

    for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : records) {
        batch.add(record.value());
    }

    if (!batch.isEmpty()) {
        // Process batch
        processBatch(batch);

        // Commit only after successful processing
        try {
            consumer.commitSync();
        } catch (CommitFailedException e) {
            // Handle: consumer lost partitions
            break;
        }
    }
}
```

#### Asynchronous Commits

```java
consumer.commitAsync()
consumer.commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback callback)
consumer.commitAsync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, OffsetCommitCallback callback)
```

**Behavior:**
- Returns immediately without waiting for broker response
- Callback invoked asynchronously when request completes
- Callback may execute in any thread (typically the poll thread)
- Earlier async commits may be superseded by later ones

**Callback Signature:**
```java
void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception)
```

**Suitable For:**
- High-throughput scenarios
- Combining with synchronous commits for periodic safety

**Pattern (Async + Periodic Sync):**
```java
int commitInterval = 100;
int recordCount = 0;

while (running) {
    ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));

    for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : records) {
        processRecord(record);
        recordCount++;

        // Async commit frequently for progress
        if (recordCount % commitInterval == 0) {
            consumer.commitAsync((offsets, exception) -> {
                if (exception != null) {
                    logger.error("Async commit failed", exception);
                }
            });
        }
    }

    // Periodic sync commits for safety
    if (recordCount % (commitInterval * 10) == 0) {
        try {
            consumer.commitSync();
        } catch (CommitFailedException e) {
            logger.error("Sync commit failed", e);
            break;
        }
    }
}
```

### Offset Queries and Seeking

#### Query Committed Offset

```java
Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> committed(Set<TopicPartition> partitions)
Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> committed(Set<TopicPartition> partitions, Duration timeout)
```

Returns the last committed offset for each partition. Useful on initialization to resume from the last committed position.

#### Query Current Position

```java
long position(TopicPartition partition)
long position(TopicPartition partition, Duration timeout)
```

Returns the offset of the next record to be fetched.

#### Seek Operations

```java
void seek(TopicPartition partition, long offset)
void seek(TopicPartition partition, OffsetAndMetadata offsetAndMetadata)
void seekToBeginning(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
void seekToEnd(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
```

**Behavior:**
- Changes the consumer's position for future `poll()` calls
- Does not fetch records immediately
- Effect visible on next `poll()`
- `seekToBeginning`: Seeks to oldest available record
- `seekToEnd`: Seeks to end of partition

**Special Case - read_committed Isolation:**
- With `isolation.level=read_committed`, `endOffsets()` returns the Last Stable Offset (LSO)
- LSO is the offset of the first message in an open transaction
- `seekToEnd()` seeks to LSO, not the true end
- Transactional messages and abort markers are filtered out

#### Offset-to-Timestamp Lookup

```java
Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> offsetsForTimes(Map<TopicPartition, Long> timestampsToSearch)
Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> offsetsForTimes(Map<TopicPartition, Long> timestampsToSearch, Duration timeout)
```

Returns the offset with a timestamp >= the specified timestamp for each partition. Useful for time-based seeking.

**Example:**
```java
// Seek to records from 5 minutes ago
long fiveMinutesAgo = System.currentTimeMillis() - 5 * 60 * 1000;
Map<TopicPartition, Long> timestamps = new HashMap<>();
for (TopicPartition tp : consumer.assignment()) {
    timestamps.put(tp, fiveMinutesAgo);
}

Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> offsets = consumer.offsetsForTimes(timestamps);
for (Map.Entry<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> entry : offsets.entrySet()) {
    if (entry.getValue() != null) {
        consumer.seek(entry.getKey(), entry.getValue().offset());
    }
}
```

### External Offset Storage

For "exactly-once" semantics with atomic processing, store offsets alongside results:

```java
while (running) {
    ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));

    // Store records and offsets atomically in external system
    for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : records) {
        database.storeResult(
            processRecord(record),
            record.offset()
        );
    }

    // Committed offset will be persisted by the database transaction
}
```

Resume from external offset on restart:
```java
consumer.assign(partitions);

for (TopicPartition tp : partitions) {
    long lastOffset = database.getLastOffset(tp);
    if (lastOffset >= 0) {
        consumer.seek(tp, lastOffset);
    } else {
        consumer.seekToBeginning(Collections.singletonList(tp));
    }
}
```

## Consumer Group Mechanics

### Group Membership and Coordination

**Group Concept:**
- Logical aggregation of consumers with the same `group.id`
- One consumer group can have multiple members
- Each partition assigned to exactly one member (for load balancing)

**Lifecycle:**
1. **Init**: Consumer created but not in group
2. **Join**: First `poll()` triggers join via JoinGroup request
3. **Sync**: Broker coordinates partition assignment
4. **Stable**: Member receives partitions and starts fetching
5. **Rebalancing**: Triggered by membership or subscription changes
6. **Revocation**: Member gives up partitions (calls `onPartitionsRevoked`)
7. **Assignment**: Member receives new partitions (calls `onPartitionsAssigned`)

### Rebalancing Details

**Rebalancing is triggered when:**
- New member joins group
- Existing member leaves/crashes
- Subscription changes (new topics match pattern)
- Topic partitions change
- Consumer calls `enforceRebalance()`

**Detection and Execution:**
- Rebalancing is **ONLY** detected during `poll()` calls
- Once detected, callbacks execute immediately within `poll()`
- No `poll()` called = no rebalance detection = potential stale assignment

**Rebalance Protocols:**

**Classic Protocol (Eager):**
- All members revoke partitions simultaneously
- `onPartitionsRevoked()` called with full assignment
- All members wait for synchronization
- Then all receive new assignments
- Higher stop-the-world pause time
- Higher latency during rebalance

**Classic Protocol (Cooperative):**
- Members retain partitions where possible
- Only revoked partitions are given up
- `onPartitionsRevoked()` called with minimal set
- Reduces stop-the-world pause
- Requires compatible assignors

**Consumer Protocol (New):**
- Always cooperative rebalancing
- Incremental partition migration
- Lowest downtime during rebalance

**Rebalance Callback Guarantees:**
- `onPartitionsRevoked()` is called before partition move
- `onPartitionsAssigned()` is called after partition is acquired
- During normal rebalance: revoked → new_owner_assigned guaranteed ordering
- Exception scenarios: partition may be lost without revoke → `onPartitionsLost()` called
- `onPartitionsAssigned()` always called (even if empty list)

### Heartbeating and Session Management

**Session Timeout** (`session.timeout.ms`, default: 10 seconds):
- If broker receives no heartbeat for this duration, consumer is removed
- Other members detect removal and rebalance

**Max Poll Interval** (`max.poll.interval.ms`, default: 5 minutes):
- If `poll()` not called for this duration, consumer proactively leaves
- Prevents livelock: consumer continues heartbeating but makes no progress
- Subsequent commits fail with `CommitFailedException`

**Heartbeat Thread:**
- Background thread sends heartbeats periodically
- Interval controlled by `heartbeat.interval.ms` (default: 3 seconds)
- Runs independently of main consumer thread

**Practical Implications:**
```java
// GOOD: Tight poll loop maintains membership
while (running) {
    ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));  // ✓ Called frequently
    for (ConsumerRecord record : records) {
        processRecord(record);  // Fast processing
    }
}

// BAD: Slow processing causes member removal
while (running) {
    ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofSeconds(30));
    for (ConsumerRecord record : records) {
        slowProcess(record);  // ✗ Can exceed max.poll.interval.ms
    }
}

// BETTER: Offload processing to thread pool
while (running) {
    ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));
    for (ConsumerRecord record : records) {
        executor.submit(() -> processRecord(record));  // Non-blocking
    }
}
```

### Enforcing Rebalance

```java
void enforceRebalance()
void enforceRebalance(String reason)
```

Manually trigger a rebalance on next `poll()`. Useful for:
- Graceful shutdown with immediate state flush
- Redistribution of partitions
- Internal consumer state reconciliation

## Flow Control and Position Management

### Pause and Resume

**Methods:**
```java
void pause(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
void resume(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions)
Set<TopicPartition> paused()
```

**Behavior:**
- `pause()`: Excludes partitions from future `poll()` calls
- Position is NOT reset (unchanged when resumed)
- Useful for flow control or batching
- Multiple pauses for different partitions are independent
- `paused()` returns the current set of paused partitions

**Use Case - Processing Coordination:**
```java
// Pause fast partition to let slow partition catch up in join
for (TopicPartition tp : consumer.assignment()) {
    long lag = endOffset - position(tp);
    if (lag > 100000) {
        consumer.resume(Collections.singletonList(tp));
    } else {
        consumer.pause(Collections.singletonList(tp));
    }
}
```

### Current Lag

```java
OptionalLong currentLag(TopicPartition partition)
```

Returns the difference between high water mark and consumer position. Useful for monitoring consumption progress.

### Partition Information

```java
List<PartitionInfo> partitionsFor(String topic)
List<PartitionInfo> partitionsFor(String topic, Duration timeout)
Map<String, List<PartitionInfo>> listTopics()
Map<String, List<PartitionInfo>> listTopics(Duration timeout)
```

Fetch metadata about topics and partitions. Blocking operations with optional timeout.

### Metrics

```java
Map<MetricName, ? extends Metric> metrics()
```

Returns all metrics being collected for this consumer. Useful for monitoring via JMX or custom collectors.

Common metrics:
- `fetch-total-bytes`
- `fetch-total-records`
- `records-lag-max`
- `records-consumed-total`
- `record-lag`

### Group Metadata

```java
ConsumerGroupMetadata groupMetadata()
```

Returns metadata about current group membership. Used in producer for transactional producing.

### Client Instance ID

```java
Uuid clientInstanceId(Duration timeout)
```

Unique identifier for this consumer instance. Used by brokers for tracking and fencing.
